_comment: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for all 5 landing-page versions. Facts/copy live here once; each bespoke page draws from this. Update numbers HERE only.

product.name: Evolve Loop
product.oneLiner: Any agent can write code. Evolve Loop is the AI-driven process that plans it, reviews it, heals its own mistakes, and decides what's safe to merge.
product.oneBreath: Your AI writes the code. A second AI reviews it. A dependable process — not an opinion — decides whether it ships.

hero.headline: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
hero.headlineLead: Ship the code your agent wrote —
headlineVariants: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
AI writes it. AI reviews it. A process you trust ships it.
An independent reviewer that won't rubber-stamp your AI.
hero.subhead: Evolve Loop wraps your AI coder in a structured, self-healing pipeline: it plans the work, an independent reviewer challenges it, only safe changes merge — and every run makes the next one smarter.

problem.heading: Agents can write the feature now. The hard part is merging it.
body: AI can write a feature, fix a bug, or refactor a module on its own. Writing code isn't the bottleneck anymore — trusting it enough to merge without reading every line is.
Most tools stop at "an AI wrote it." The hard part is everything after: was it the right change, is it actually correct, and will it break something downstream?

theTurn.heading: Intelligence proposes. A dependable process decides.
body: Deciding what to build, how to build it, and what looks wrong is where AI shines — so the pipeline lets it make those calls freely.
What's safe to ship is decided by a structured, repeatable process with guardrails the AI can't talk its way past. Freedom where it helps, structure where it counts.

phaseSpine.blurb: Turns a vague ask into a goal everyone agrees on — with the risky assumptions named up front.
Explores the code and past lessons, then proposes what's actually worth doing.
Decides what makes this cycle and what waits — so scope never sprawls.
Implements the change in isolation, with tests written right alongside it.
A different reviewer challenges the work and decides whether it holds up.
Merges only what passed — cleanly, and on the record.
Turns the outcome into a durable lesson the next cycle starts with.

pipelineDemo.heading: The model composes its own pipeline.
pipelineDemo.sub: Give it a goal. The advisor runs the mandated fundamentals, picks from the common phases, and writes brand-new ones when the goal needs them — then routes each phase to the right LLM and model: Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini, per policy. The combinations are unlimited; nothing here is pre-scripted.

cases.goal: Build a `<canvas>` Snake game web page

phases.why: a vague one-liner — pin scope before any code
no game code yet — fast survey of the stack, pick canvas
split board · snake · food · loop · score
a new UI surface — Design + Eng lenses weigh in
encode the rules: growth, collision, wrap, lose
fundamental — implement loop + input + render
fundamental — a different family than the builder, so the review stays honest
no phase judges whether the controls feel right — so the advisor writes one
60fps is trivial here — perf isn't the goal
no auth, no input, no data — zero surface
fundamental — gated commit once the evals are green
PASS → memo the canvas game-loop pattern
the goal is exact — nothing to clarify
find every reference so the rename is total
one mechanical change — nothing to defer
a pure rename — no design surface
behavior is unchanged — existing tests pin it
fundamental — apply it; the compiler finds stragglers
fundamental — a second family runs the suite, proving nothing moved
identical runtime behavior
no security surface touched
fundamental — gated commit + push
trivial — no lesson worth keeping

cases.goal: Fix the off-by-one crashing the last page of results

phases.why: the bug report is concrete — the goal is clear
find where the page boundary is computed
the advisor writes a phase to pin the exact off-by-one before any fix
a single defect — nothing to split
no new design — behavior already specified
write a test that crashes on the last page first
fundamental — fix the index math → GREEN
fundamental — a different family replays the repro + full regression
the fix is O(1) — no perf impact
no security surface in pagination math
fundamental — gated commit + push
PASS → memo the boundary-test pattern

cases.goal: Add email + password login with Stripe checkout

phases.why: auth + payments — pin the flows + failure modes
survey session + DB layer; check the Stripe SDK
sequence: auth → checkout → webhooks
all four lenses — the Security lens is mandatory
encode success + every rejection path as RED
fundamental — implement auth + checkout → GREEN
fundamental — a different family than the builder audits the code
scored: hashing, authz, secrets, webhook sigs
payments need an idempotency/replay guard no common phase covers — minted
correctness over latency at this stage
fundamental — gated commit once the security score clears
PASS → memo the auth + webhook pattern

cases.goal: Update the README install section for the new installer

phases.why: a doc change — the scope is obvious
the change is the prose itself — no code to map
a single doc edit
prose, not architecture
no code — nothing to test
fundamental — rewrite the section; verify the command runs
fundamental — doc-lint + link-check
no runtime path
no security surface
fundamental — gated commit + push
no engineering lesson

cases.goal: Speed up the dashboard query that times out on big tenants

phases.why: the target is measurable — make it fast
profile the slow path — find the N+1 / missing index
a single hotspot
no behavior change — just faster
characterization tests — the output must not change
fundamental — add the index / batch the query
fundamental — a different family checks outputs are identical
the advisor writes a phase to diff the SQL plan before vs after
measure before/after — prove + guard the speedup
query shape unchanged — no new exposure
fundamental — gated commit once the benchmark clears
PASS → memo the indexing fix

pillarsIntro.heading: Designed like a team, not a one-shot prompt.

pillars.title: AI-driven, end to end
pillars.claim: It designs the work — it doesn't just type it.
pillars.proof: The pipeline decides what to build, how to approach it, and what's risky. Real judgment at every step, instead of one prompt fired into the dark.

pillars.title: A pipeline that adapts
pillars.claim: Structure that reshapes itself to the task.
pillars.proof: Every change follows the same clear pipeline — but it flexes. A one-line fix skips the ceremony; a risky feature earns extra review. Structured enough to trust, dynamic enough to stay fast.

pillars.title: Agile by nature
pillars.claim: Plan, build, review, reflect — every cycle.
pillars.proof: Each cycle runs like a sprint: scope the work, build it, review it, ship it, then learn. Scope stays bounded, so nothing sprawls and progress compounds.

pillars.title: Self-healing
pillars.claim: Failures don't derail it — they teach it.
pillars.proof: When something breaks, the pipeline catches it, recovers, and turns the failure into a lasting rule. The same mistake doesn't get to happen twice.

pillars.title: An independent second mind
pillars.claim: Built to challenge the work, not rubber-stamp it.
pillars.proof: A separate reviewer — a different model entirely — tries to break each change before it ships. No one grading their own homework.

pillars.title: Any model. No lock-in.
pillars.claim: The best LLM for each job — mix and match.
pillars.proof: Run Claude, Gemini, and Codex side by side and route the strongest model to each role. Switch whenever you like; your process never depends on one vendor.

incident.heading: The system caught its own mistakes.
incident.body: An experiment with a new model slipped several regressions into a single run. Not one reached a human — the pipeline's own reviews caught every one, recovered, and within two days turned each into a permanent safeguard. That's the whole idea: runs can fail, but failures become fixes that stick.

comparison.heading: When to reach for it.
rows: It plans and scopes the work
An independent review has to pass
Compounds — every run teaches the next
Any model — the best one per job
Work you actually have to merge
comparison.note: Think of the agent as the developer, and Evolve Loop as the team and process around it — the planning, the review, and the discipline that make the output safe to ship.

quickstart.heading: Your first cycle is one command away.

examples.heading: From one command to full control.
examples.sub: Start with a goal. Add a flag only when you want more — here's what each does behind the scenes.

items.label: Just a goal
items.note: The advisor composes the whole pipeline — which phases run, which LLM runs each, and how many cycles. It stops when the work is done.

items.label: Steer the approach
items.note: A strategy (harden · repair · innovate · balanced) shifts scope and strictness — same phase spine, different posture.

items.label: Bound the cycles
items.note: --cycles N is a hard contract: exactly N cycles, never early-stopped.

items.label: Control the models
items.note: Pick a preset once; it routes each phase to an LLM (e.g. Build on Codex, Audit on Claude) in .evolve/policy.json.

items.label: Resume
items.note: Picks a checkpointed run up exactly where it stopped — worktree and state intact, no work lost.

setup.heading: Setup is one command — and optional
setup.note: Run once: it detects your installed LLM CLIs and offers three presets. One choice writes per-phase model routing to .evolve/policy.json. Skip it entirely and the loop runs with sensible all-Claude defaults — most people never open the file.

concurrency.heading: Run several loops at once.
concurrency.sub: Give each feature its own loop and they run side by side — each in its own git worktree and branch, each on its own LLM, each composing the pipeline its goal needs. They never step on each other, and only green changes merge to main, one at a time.

scenarios.title: Many features at once
scenarios.note: Point a loop at each goal; they advance in parallel, not in a queue.

scenarios.title: Isolated by design
scenarios.note: Each loop owns a worktree + branch — no crossed wires, no half-merges.

scenarios.title: Safe, serialized merges
scenarios.note: Only green work reaches main, one merge at a time.

finalCta.heading: Stop reading diffs you didn't write.
finalCta.subhead: Open source. Apache-2.0. Works with Claude, Gemini, and Codex.

footer.grounding: Grounded in reflective self-improvement and double-loop learning.
